channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: What People Regret Throwing Away During Decluttering in London
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Many London homeowners embarking on a major decluttering spree later find themselves wishing they hadn't been quite so ruthless. Whether preparing for a house move, tackling a loft clearance, or simply craving more living space, residents across the capital frequently report regretting items they hastily discarded during the process.

Common Decluttering Regrets

Property owners often realise too late that certain possessions held more value than they initially thought. Typical regrets include:

  • Sentimental items like childhood memorabilia or family heirlooms
  • Vintage furniture that could have been restored or repurposed
  • Books and vinyl records that later become sought-after collectors' items
  • Quality tools or DIY equipment that need replacing for home improvements

The emotional attachment to belongings often becomes clearer after they're gone, particularly when clearing a property after major life events like relationship breakdowns or bereavement. Many find that what seemed like clutter in the moment actually represented important personal history.

Making Smarter Decluttering Decisions

For those considering a major clear-out, it could be worth implementing a more measured approach:

  • Creating a "maybe" pile to revisit after a cooling-off period
  • Researching whether items have financial value before disposal
  • Considering professional valuation for antiques or collectables
  • Exploring storage solutions for seasonal or occasional-use items

London's competitive property market means many residents live in compact spaces, making thoughtful decluttering particularly important. Whilst creating more living space remains valuable, keeping hold of meaningful or potentially valuable possessions might save future regrets.

Homeowners facing storage challenges might explore creative solutions like under-bed storage, wall-mounted shelving, or multi-functional furniture before opting for wholesale clearances. Taking time to assess each item's true worth could prevent that all-too-common decluttering remorse.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Many London homeowners embarking on a major decluttering spree later find themselves wishing they hadn't been quite so ruthless. Whether preparing for a house move, tackling a loft clearance, or simply craving more living space, residents across the capital frequently report regretting items they hastily discarded during the process.

Common Decluttering Regrets

Property owners often realise too late that certain possessions held more value than they initially thought. Typical regrets include:

  • Sentimental items like childhood memorabilia or family heirlooms
  • Vintage furniture that could have been restored or repurposed
  • Books and vinyl records that later become sought-after collectors' items
  • Quality tools or DIY equipment that need replacing for home improvements

The emotional attachment to belongings often becomes clearer after they're gone, particularly when clearing a property after major life events like relationship breakdowns or bereavement. Many find that what seemed like clutter in the moment actually represented important personal history.

Making Smarter Decluttering Decisions

For those considering a major clear-out, it could be worth implementing a more measured approach:

  • Creating a "maybe" pile to revisit after a cooling-off period
  • Researching whether items have financial value before disposal
  • Considering professional valuation for antiques or collectables
  • Exploring storage solutions for seasonal or occasional-use items

London's competitive property market means many residents live in compact spaces, making thoughtful decluttering particularly important. Whilst creating more living space remains valuable, keeping hold of meaningful or potentially valuable possessions might save future regrets.

Homeowners facing storage challenges might explore creative solutions like under-bed storage, wall-mounted shelving, or multi-functional furniture before opting for wholesale clearances. Taking time to assess each item's true worth could prevent that all-too-common decluttering remorse.

item.title: Rents rise in May but year-on-year growth remains subdued
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

May saw a modest increase in rental prices across the UK, though annual growth remains relatively low. This subdued trend comes as the market continues to adjust to the implications of the Renters’ Rights Act, introduced earlier this year. Landlords and tenants alike are navigating the changes, with the legislation aiming to provide greater protections for renters whilst balancing the interests of property investors.

Monthly Rent Increases

Data from recent reports highlights a slight uptick in rental prices during May. Whilst this rise may indicate a gradual recovery in the rental market, the increase remains marginal. Factors such as seasonal demand and a stabilising housing market could be contributing to this trend. However, the overall pace of growth suggests that the market is still finding its footing after a period of uncertainty.

Impact of the Renters’ Rights Act

The Renters’ Rights Act, which came into effect earlier this year, has brought significant changes to the rental landscape. Designed to enhance tenant protections, the legislation includes measures such as stricter regulations on evictions and improved standards for rental properties. Whilst these changes are welcomed by tenants, landlords may find themselves adapting to new responsibilities and compliance requirements. This transitional phase could be influencing the slower annual growth in rental prices, as both parties adjust to the evolving framework.

Homeowners and property investors might consider reviewing their strategies in light of these developments. For landlords, ensuring compliance with the new regulations could help maintain tenant satisfaction and reduce potential disputes. Tenants, on the other hand, may benefit from understanding their enhanced rights under the Act, enabling them to make informed decisions when renting properties.

Looking Ahead

As the rental market continues to adapt, it remains to be seen how these trends will evolve in the coming months. Whilst monthly increases in rent suggest a degree of stability, the subdued annual growth indicates that the market is still in a state of flux. Both landlords and tenants may wish to stay informed about further developments, particularly as the long-term effects of the Renters’ Rights Act become clearer.

For those navigating the rental market, seeking professional advice could prove valuable. Mortgage brokers, letting agents, and legal advisors may provide insights tailored to individual circumstances, helping both landlords and tenants make the most of the current landscape.

By Nadya Putri Maharani

May saw a modest increase in rental prices across the UK, though annual growth remains relatively low. This subdued trend comes as the market continues to adjust to the implications of the Renters’ Rights Act, introduced earlier this year. Landlords and tenants alike are navigating the changes, with the legislation aiming to provide greater protections for renters whilst balancing the interests of property investors.

Data from recent reports highlights a slight uptick in rental prices during May. Whilst this rise may indicate a gradual recovery in the rental market, the increase remains marginal. Factors such as seasonal demand and a stabilising housing market could be contributing to this trend. However, the overall pace of growth suggests that the market is still finding its footing after a period of uncertainty.

Impact of the Renters’ Rights Act

The Renters’ Rights Act, which came into effect earlier this year, has brought significant changes to the rental landscape. Designed to enhance tenant protections, the legislation includes measures such as stricter regulations on evictions and improved standards for rental properties. Whilst these changes are welcomed by tenants, landlords may find themselves adapting to new responsibilities and compliance requirements. This transitional phase could be influencing the slower annual growth in rental prices, as both parties adjust to the evolving framework.

Homeowners and property investors might consider reviewing their strategies in light of these developments. For landlords, ensuring compliance with the new regulations could help maintain tenant satisfaction and reduce potential disputes. Tenants, on the other hand, may benefit from understanding their enhanced rights under the Act, enabling them to make informed decisions when renting properties.

Looking Ahead

As the rental market continues to adapt, it remains to be seen how these trends will evolve in the coming months. Whilst monthly increases in rent suggest a degree of stability, the subdued annual growth indicates that the market is still in a state of flux. Both landlords and tenants may wish to stay informed about further developments, particularly as the long-term effects of the Renters’ Rights Act become clearer.

For those navigating the rental market, seeking professional advice could prove valuable. Mortgage brokers, letting agents, and legal advisors may provide insights tailored to individual circumstances, helping both landlords and tenants make the most of the current landscape.

item.title: London sees 69% premium on average rents
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

London's rental market continues to command significantly higher prices than the national average, with new figures revealing a striking 69% premium compared to typical UK rents. The capital's average monthly rent now stands at £2,339, reflecting sustained demand from tenants across all boroughs.

What's driving London's rental premium?

Several factors contribute to London's substantial rental premium:

  • Consistently high demand from professionals and international tenants
  • Limited housing stock relative to population growth
  • Premium prices for properties near transport hubs and business districts
  • Ongoing return-to-office trends boosting central London demand

Implications for renters and landlords

For tenants considering a move to the capital, these figures highlight the importance of budgeting carefully for housing costs. Those relocating for work might wish to explore:

  • Commuter towns with lower rents but good transport links
  • House shares to split costs in premium locations
  • Negotiating longer tenancies to lock in current rates

For landlords and property investors, London's rental market continues to present opportunities, though it's worth noting that higher rents often come with increased tenant expectations regarding property standards and location amenities.

As the market evolves, both renters and property owners might benefit from staying informed about local rental trends and considering professional advice when making housing decisions in the capital.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

London's rental market continues to command significantly higher prices than the national average, with new figures revealing a striking 69% premium compared to typical UK rents. The capital's average monthly rent now stands at £2,339, reflecting sustained demand from tenants across all boroughs.

What's driving London's rental premium?

Several factors contribute to London's substantial rental premium:

  • Consistently high demand from professionals and international tenants
  • Limited housing stock relative to population growth
  • Premium prices for properties near transport hubs and business districts
  • Ongoing return-to-office trends boosting central London demand

Implications for renters and landlords

  • Commuter towns with lower rents but good transport links
  • House shares to split costs in premium locations
  • Negotiating longer tenancies to lock in current rates

For landlords and property investors, London's rental market continues to present opportunities, though it's worth noting that higher rents often come with increased tenant expectations regarding property standards and location amenities.

As the market evolves, both renters and property owners might benefit from staying informed about local rental trends and considering professional advice when making housing decisions in the capital.

item.title: Auctions generate four times number of complaints
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Property auctions are facing scrutiny as new data reveals they generate four times as many complaints as traditional property transactions. Industry experts are calling for improved transparency and clearer guidance for buyers navigating the fast-paced auction environment.

Understanding the complaint disparity

The significant gap in complaints between auction purchases and conventional property sales has raised eyebrows across the sector. Several factors may contribute to this trend:

  • The binding nature of auction contracts leaves little room for reconsideration after the hammer falls
  • Limited time for due diligence compared to standard property purchases
  • Potential misunderstandings about auction terms and conditions
  • The accelerated process may catch some buyers unprepared for the financial commitments

Moving towards better auction practices

Industry bodies are now examining ways to improve the auction experience whilst maintaining its efficiency. Some proposed measures include:

  • Standardised documentation to make terms clearer for all parties
  • Enhanced pre-auction information packs to help buyers make informed decisions
  • Better signposting of financial implications and timescales
  • Improved complaints handling procedures specific to auction transactions

For those considering property auctions, it could be worth thoroughly researching the process and seeking professional advice before participating. The speed and finality of auction purchases mean preparation is key to avoiding potential pitfalls.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Property auctions are facing scrutiny as new data reveals they generate four times as many complaints as traditional property transactions. Industry experts are calling for improved transparency and clearer guidance for buyers navigating the fast-paced auction environment.

Understanding the complaint disparity

The significant gap in complaints between auction purchases and conventional property sales has raised eyebrows across the sector. Several factors may contribute to this trend:

  • The binding nature of auction contracts leaves little room for reconsideration after the hammer falls
  • Limited time for due diligence compared to standard property purchases
  • Potential misunderstandings about auction terms and conditions
  • The accelerated process may catch some buyers unprepared for the financial commitments

Moving towards better auction practices

Industry bodies are now examining ways to improve the auction experience whilst maintaining its efficiency. Some proposed measures include:

  • Standardised documentation to make terms clearer for all parties
  • Enhanced pre-auction information packs to help buyers make informed decisions
  • Better signposting of financial implications and timescales
  • Improved complaints handling procedures specific to auction transactions

For those considering property auctions, it could be worth thoroughly researching the process and seeking professional advice before participating. The speed and finality of auction purchases mean preparation is key to avoiding potential pitfalls.

item.title: Chestertons Global launches new Marbella division
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Chestertons Global has announced the launch of a new division in Marbella, marking its latest expansion into the international luxury property market. The move comes as part of a strategic partnership aimed at catering to high-net-worth individuals and investors seeking premium real estate opportunities in the coveted Spanish coastal destination.

Focus on Marbella’s Luxury Market

Marbella, renowned for its glamorous lifestyle and picturesque Mediterranean setting, has long been a hotspot for luxury property buyers. Chestertons Global’s new division will focus on meeting the growing demand for high-end homes in the area, offering tailored services to clients looking to purchase or invest in exclusive properties. This expansion reflects the firm’s commitment to strengthening its presence in key international markets.

Partnership to Enhance Services

The launch of the Marbella division is the result of a collaboration with a local partner, combining Chestertons Global’s international expertise with in-depth regional knowledge. This partnership is expected to provide clients with seamless access to a wide range of luxury properties, from beachfront villas to modern penthouses, alongside expert advice on market trends and investment opportunities.

For homeowners or investors considering Marbella as a destination, working with an established agency like Chestertons Global could offer peace of mind and access to a diverse portfolio of premium properties. The firm’s reputation for delivering personalised service and its global network may prove valuable for those navigating the luxury real estate market.

As Chestertons Global continues to expand its footprint, its new Marbella division highlights the enduring appeal of the Costa del Sol for international buyers and investors. With its blend of natural beauty, upscale amenities, and strong investment potential, Marbella remains a prime location for luxury property transactions.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The move comes as part of a strategic partnership aimed at catering to high-net-worth individuals and investors seeking premium real estate opportunities in the coveted Spanish coastal destination.

This partnership is expected to provide clients with seamless access to a wide range of luxury properties, from beachfront villas to modern penthouses, alongside expert advice on market trends and investment opportunities.

For homeowners or investors considering Marbella as a destination, working with an established agency like Chestertons Global could offer peace of mind and access to a diverse portfolio of premium properties. The firm’s reputation for delivering personalised service and its global network may prove valuable for those navigating the luxury real estate market.

As Chestertons Global continues to expand its footprint, its new Marbella division highlights the enduring appeal of the Costa del Sol for international buyers and investors. With its blend of natural beauty, upscale amenities, and strong investment potential, Marbella remains a prime location for luxury property transactions.

item.title: Major estate agency strikes fresh acquisition deal
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Dexters expands London footprint with Fraser & Co acquisition

One of London's largest estate agency groups has strengthened its presence across prime property markets with the acquisition of long-established firm Fraser & Co. The deal adds four London offices and a Hong Kong operation to Dexters' growing network, bringing over 30 experienced employees into the fold.

Strategic expansion in key locations

The acquisition sees Dexters absorb Fraser & Co's offices in some of London's most desirable postcodes, including the West End, City & Shoreditch areas, alongside Kew Bridge & Brentford and Colindale locations. The Paddington-based property management division also transfers as part of the deal.

For homeowners and investors in these areas, the merger could mean access to Dexters' wider resources whilst maintaining Fraser & Co's local market expertise. The Hong Kong connection may particularly interest international buyers looking at London property, as it strengthens Dexters' overseas service capabilities.

Combining decades of property expertise

Founded in 1995, Fraser & Co has built a strong reputation in prime London markets and new-build developments. Their integration into Dexters creates one of the capital's most extensive property networks, with the combined entity now operating from over 180 offices and managing 50,000 rental properties.

Dexters CEO Ash Kashyap highlighted Fraser & Co's journey from family business to respected industry player, stating the acquisition aligns with their strategy of combining local knowledge with corporate resources. Meanwhile, Fraser & Co's managing director Robert Fraser pointed to Dexters' market-leading position as a key factor in the decision to join forces.

For those considering property transactions in affected areas, the merger may bring benefits through enhanced services and potentially greater market coverage. However, as with any major industry consolidation, borrowers and sellers might wish to monitor how the changes affect local competition and service offerings.

By Bambang Setiawan

Dexters expands London footprint with Fraser & Co acquisition

One of London's largest estate agency groups has strengthened its presence across prime property markets with the acquisition of long-established firm Fraser & Co. The deal adds four London offices and a Hong Kong operation to Dexters' growing network, bringing over 30 experienced employees into the fold.

The acquisition sees Dexters absorb Fraser & Co's offices in some of London's most desirable postcodes, including the West End, City & Shoreditch areas, alongside Kew Bridge & Brentford and Colindale locations. The Paddington-based property management division also transfers as part of the deal.

For homeowners and investors in these areas, the merger could mean access to Dexters' wider resources whilst maintaining Fraser & Co's local market expertise. The Hong Kong connection may particularly interest international buyers looking at London property, as it strengthens Dexters' overseas service capabilities.

Combining decades of property expertise

Founded in 1995, Fraser & Co has built a strong reputation in prime London markets and new-build developments. Their integration into Dexters creates one of the capital's most extensive property networks, with the combined entity now operating from over 180 offices and managing 50,000 rental properties.

Dexters CEO Ash Kashyap highlighted Fraser & Co's journey from family business to respected industry player, stating the acquisition aligns with their strategy of combining local knowledge with corporate resources. For those considering property transactions in affected areas, the merger may bring benefits through enhanced services and potentially greater market coverage. However, as with any major industry consolidation, borrowers and sellers might wish to monitor how the changes affect local competition and service offerings.

item.title: Ground rent cap to be brought forward by a year
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The government has announced plans to fast-track its proposed ground rent cap, with the £250 limit now expected to take effect in late 2027 – a full year earlier than initially proposed. This acceleration comes amid mounting political pressure and follows recommendations from parliamentary committees calling for swifter leasehold reforms to protect homeowners.

What the changes mean for leaseholders

The forthcoming legislation, currently in draft form, aims to significantly reduce financial burdens for leasehold property owners across England and Wales. Under the proposals:

  • Ground rents would be capped at £250 annually
  • Future increases would be effectively frozen in real terms
  • Leaseholders could save thousands over the duration of their leases

The reforms form part of a broader package of measures designed to rebalance the relationship between leaseholders and freeholders. Housing experts suggest this represents the most substantial intervention in the leasehold sector for decades.

Political momentum behind the reforms

The revised timetable follows sustained pressure from backbench MPs and housing campaigners, with the Housing, Communities and Local Government Committee recently urging ministers to implement changes without delay. Key developments include:

  • The draft Bill published in January 2024
  • Plans to introduce final legislation before Parliament's summer recess
  • Cross-party support for faster implementation of leasehold reforms

A government spokesperson confirmed the accelerated schedule, stating the measures would deliver meaningful financial relief to millions of homeowners currently facing escalating ground rent charges.

With the legislation now moving through Parliament, leaseholders may wish to monitor developments closely, as these reforms could substantially alter the long-term costs associated with leasehold properties. The changes are particularly significant for those considering property purchases or lease extensions in the coming years.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The government has announced plans to fast-track its proposed ground rent cap, with the £250 limit now expected to take effect in late 2027 – a full year earlier than initially proposed. This acceleration comes amid mounting political pressure and follows recommendations from parliamentary committees calling for swifter leasehold reforms to protect homeowners.

What the changes mean for leaseholders

The forthcoming legislation, currently in draft form, aims to significantly reduce financial burdens for leasehold property owners across England and Wales. Under the proposals:

  • Ground rents would be capped at £250 annually
  • Future increases would be effectively frozen in real terms
  • Leaseholders could save thousands over the duration of their leases

The reforms form part of a broader package of measures designed to rebalance the relationship between leaseholders and freeholders. Housing experts suggest this represents the most substantial intervention in the leasehold sector for decades.

Political momentum behind the reforms

The revised timetable follows sustained pressure from backbench MPs and housing campaigners, with the Housing, Communities and Local Government Committee recently urging ministers to implement changes without delay. Key developments include:

  • The draft Bill published in January 2024
  • Plans to introduce final legislation before Parliament's summer recess
  • Cross-party support for faster implementation of leasehold reforms

A government spokesperson confirmed the accelerated schedule, stating the measures would deliver meaningful financial relief to millions of homeowners currently facing escalating ground rent charges.

With the legislation now moving through Parliament, leaseholders may wish to monitor developments closely, as these reforms could substantially alter the long-term costs associated with leasehold properties. The changes are particularly significant for those considering property purchases or lease extensions in the coming years.

item.title: Housing minister to deliver government housing update at Propertymark One
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Property professionals may wish to note that Baroness Taylor, the government’s housing spokesperson in the House of Lords, is set to deliver a keynote address at the Propertymark One conference this Friday. The event at London’s ExCeL will provide insights into upcoming housing policy reforms and their potential impact on the UK property market.

Key Policy Updates for the Property Sector

As Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Housing and Local Government, Baroness Taylor is expected to outline the government’s current priorities for the housing market. These may include:

  • Proposed changes to the home buying and selling process
  • New requirements for material information in property listings
  • Ongoing regulatory reforms affecting estate agents and conveyancers

The conference comes at a time when the property sector faces significant transformation, with technological advancements and shifting consumer expectations driving change across the industry.

Wider Conference Programme Highlights

Alongside the ministerial update, Propertymark One will feature discussions on several pressing industry topics:

  • Improving transaction speeds in the property market
  • The growing role of AI and proptech in real estate
  • Emerging business models for estate agencies
  • Current recruitment challenges in the sector

Other notable speakers include business figures Karren Brady and Daisy McAndrew, alongside politicians Mel Stride and Angela Rayner, making this a significant gathering for property professionals seeking to understand future market directions.

With housing policy reforms potentially affecting everything from mortgage applications to property transactions, industry professionals might consider such events valuable for staying informed about upcoming changes. The conference represents an opportunity to gain direct insight into government thinking at a time of considerable evolution for the UK property market.

By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Property professionals may wish to note that Baroness Taylor, the government’s housing spokesperson in the House of Lords, is set to deliver a keynote address at the Propertymark One conference this Friday. Key Policy Updates for the Property Sector

As Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Housing and Local Government, Baroness Taylor is expected to outline the government’s current priorities for the housing market. These may include:

  • Proposed changes to the home buying and selling process
  • New requirements for material information in property listings
  • Ongoing regulatory reforms affecting estate agents and conveyancers

The conference comes at a time when the property sector faces significant transformation, with technological advancements and shifting consumer expectations driving change across the industry.

Wider Conference Programme Highlights

  • Improving transaction speeds in the property market
  • The growing role of AI and proptech in real estate
  • Emerging business models for estate agencies
  • Current recruitment challenges in the sector

Other notable speakers include business figures Karren Brady and Daisy McAndrew, alongside politicians Mel Stride and Angela Rayner, making this a significant gathering for property professionals seeking to understand future market directions.

With housing policy reforms potentially affecting everything from mortgage applications to property transactions, industry professionals might consider such events valuable for staying informed about upcoming changes. The conference represents an opportunity to gain direct insight into government thinking at a time of considerable evolution for the UK property market.

item.title: More than half of first-time buyers rely on family cash
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

New research from Savills reveals that over half of first-time buyers in the UK now depend on financial assistance from family members to purchase their first home. The study highlights how rising property prices and higher mortgage rates have made it increasingly difficult for new entrants to the housing market without family support.

The growing role of family support

According to the findings, 53% of first-time buyers received some form of financial help from relatives in 2025, with family contributions totalling an estimated £11bn. This includes £8.3bn in direct financial support, demonstrating the significant role played by what's often termed the 'Bank of Mum and Dad'.

The research shows that gifts remain the most common form of assistance, with 32% of buyers receiving money from family members. Meanwhile, 16% relied on loans from relatives, and 14% used inherited wealth to fund their purchase. Interestingly, support is increasingly coming from extended family networks rather than just parents.

Challenges in the current market

With higher mortgage rates stretching affordability, the average deposit required remains substantial. Savills notes that while 64% of first-time buyers still use their own savings towards deposits, family assistance has become crucial for many to bridge the gap.

Lucian Cook, Savills' head of residential research, commented: "First-time buyer activity has remained more resilient than anticipated, but the outlook remains challenging in the current interest rate environment. While gradual rate reductions and less stringent mortgage regulation may improve access to homeownership, family support will likely continue playing a vital role."

The research also found that only 12% of first-time buyers used government-backed homeownership schemes, suggesting these initiatives may not be reaching as many potential homeowners as intended.

As the property market continues to evolve, first-time buyers might consider exploring all available options, from family assistance to government schemes, when planning their route onto the property ladder.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

New research from Savills reveals that over half of first-time buyers in the UK now depend on financial assistance from family members to purchase their first home. The study highlights how rising property prices and higher mortgage rates have made it increasingly difficult for new entrants to the housing market without family support.

The growing role of family support

According to the findings, 53% of first-time buyers received some form of financial help from relatives in 2025, with family contributions totalling an estimated £11bn. This includes £8.3bn in direct financial support, demonstrating the significant role played by what's often termed the 'Bank of Mum and Dad'.

The research shows that gifts remain the most common form of assistance, with 32% of buyers receiving money from family members. Meanwhile, 16% relied on loans from relatives, and 14% used inherited wealth to fund their purchase. Interestingly, support is increasingly coming from extended family networks rather than just parents.

Challenges in the current market

With higher mortgage rates stretching affordability, the average deposit required remains substantial. Savills notes that while 64% of first-time buyers still use their own savings towards deposits, family assistance has become crucial for many to bridge the gap.

Lucian Cook, Savills' head of residential research, commented: "First-time buyer activity has remained more resilient than anticipated, but the outlook remains challenging in the current interest rate environment. While gradual rate reductions and less stringent mortgage regulation may improve access to homeownership, family support will likely continue playing a vital role."

The research also found that only 12% of first-time buyers used government-backed homeownership schemes, suggesting these initiatives may not be reaching as many potential homeowners as intended.

As the property market continues to evolve, first-time buyers might consider exploring all available options, from family assistance to government schemes, when planning their route onto the property ladder.

item.title: Halifax figures show it remains a buyers’ market
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

House prices dip further as buyers gain advantage

Recent data from Halifax indicates UK property prices continued to soften in May, reinforcing current conditions that favour purchasers in the housing market. This downward trend presents potential opportunities for both first-time buyers and those looking to move up the property ladder.

What the figures reveal

The latest Halifax House Price Index shows:

  • A continued moderation in property values across most UK regions
  • Improved affordability relative to recent market peaks
  • Increased negotiating power for prospective buyers

Market analysts suggest this cooling follows the Bank of England's series of interest rate increases, which have gradually tempered demand after the pandemic-driven property boom. However, regional variations remain significant, with some areas seeing more pronounced price adjustments than others.

Considerations for potential buyers

For those contemplating a property purchase in the current climate:

  • Mortgage rates, whilst higher than during the record-low period, have stabilised somewhat recently
  • Lenders may be more open to negotiation on both property prices and mortgage terms
  • Longer fixed-rate mortgage deals could provide certainty against potential future base rate changes

Prospective buyers might wish to consult independent mortgage brokers to explore the full range of options available in today's market. With careful financial planning and professional advice, current conditions could present favourable opportunities for those well-positioned to proceed.
item.content:encoded:

House prices dip further as buyers gain advantage
